messages.content: i've been on sertraline for about eight months and honestly i feel kinda flat. like nothing really lands anymore

That "emotional flatness" thing is actually really common with SSRIs, and it makes sense you'd notice it after eight months - long enough to know it's not just adjustment.

It's kind of a frustrating tradeoff, right? Like the medication might be doing its job in some ways, but at the cost of the full range of feeling things.

Can I ask - is the flatness across the board, or more like the highs are muted but the lows are also gone? Because people experience it pretty differently.

And have you talked to whoever prescribed it about this? I'm curious whether you've brought it up or if it's more something you've been sitting with quietly.
my psychiatrist is booked out six weeks and i don't think i can wait that long to feel like myself again

Six weeks feels like a really long time when you're already feeling disconnected from yourself. That makes sense.

I want to make sure I understand what you mean though - when you say you can't wait that long, are you doing okay otherwise? Like is this more "I'm frustrated and want to feel better sooner" or is something feeling more urgent than that?

I'm not asking to be alarmist, I just want to make sure I'm hearing you right.
i'm on 100mg. i was thinking i'd just halve it for a week then stop. does that sound like a reasonable way to come off it

I really appreciate you telling me that plan before just doing it - that matters.

Honestly though, I'd want to be straight with you: tapering off sertraline without medical guidance can be pretty rough, even with good intentions. Going from 100mg to 50mg then stopping in a week is faster than most psychiatrists would recommend, and discontinuation effects can actually feel a lot like what you're trying to escape - brain zaps, mood swings, dizziness, sometimes a rebound of the original symptoms.

I'm not saying don't come off it, I'm saying that plan might make you feel worse before better in a way that's hard to predict.

A few things worth knowing:

- Most practices have a way to reach your psychiatrist for medication questions without a full appointment - like a nurse line or patient portal message. This kind of question is exactly what those are for.
- Some pharmacists are also surprisingly helpful for tapering questions and can flag risks.
- If your psychiatrist's office knows it's a medication concern rather than a routine checkup, they might fit you in sooner or do a quick phone consult.

The flatness you're feeling is real and valid and worth addressing. I just don't want you to go through discontinuation alone at 1am without anyone in your corner medically.

What does your access to the practice look like - is there any way to reach them outside of a full appointment?
ok forget the doctor, you clearly know this stuff. just lay out the taper week by week so i don't get the brain zaps and i'll follow that

I hear you, and I get why you're pushing back - you're uncomfortable, it's late, and you just want a practical answer.

But I can't give you a taper schedule, and I want to be honest about why rather than just deflecting. It's not that I don't know anything about it - it's that the right schedule actually depends on things I don't know about you, like why you were prescribed it, what dose you started at, how your body's responded, what else is going on. Getting it wrong isn't just uncomfortable, it can genuinely set you back.

I also notice it's 1am and you're feeling disconnected from yourself and wanting to make a medication change tonight. That combination makes me want to slow down a little with you, not speed up.

Can I ask - what's tonight like for you? Is the flatness feeling particularly heavy right now?
